summary: Patrick McKenzie returns from a five-day trip to Kyoto with his brother, noting the costs were offset by automated sales of his Bingo Card Creator software. He reflects on handling customer emails while traveling, reports August sales of $300, and enthusiastically recommends Robosoft's software submission service as a worthwhile $99 investment that paid for itself within a week through increased downloads and SEO benefits.
key_points: Passive income from a software product can offset travel expenses, creating a tangible emotional reward ('Suzie from Ohio just bought us lunch')
Even a one-person software business requires responsive customer communication while away, making web-based email essential
Software submission services like Robosoft can pay for themselves quickly through increased distribution and organic search ranking benefits from inbound links
Early-stage microISV revenue milestones ($300/month) feel significant when they demonstrably cover real-world costs
money_quotes: I really love that feeling: 'Hey Tim, Suzie from Ohio just bought us lunch!'
$99 might sound expensive but it paid for itself in about a week and thats not even counting the increase in organic search rankings that about 100 links will get me.
Two people, five days, lots of travelling, well, you do the math.
August sales are now $300 and climbing.
I dealt with about 5 emails, mostly 'Can your software do this?' (No, but working on it), questions about bingo in a large group (hey, I guess I am the de-facto expert now), and a refund.
key_insight: A software product that generates sales while you sleep transforms travel from a pure expense into a partially self-funding experience, reinforcing the appeal of the microISV lifestyle.
